attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old man who recently migrated from Somalia presents to the General Practice clinic with a painless 2 cm lump in his neck which he first noticed six weeks ago. During this time he has experienced night sweats, increasing fatigue, and 5 kg weight loss. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a fluctuant non-tender lump in his left supraclavicular fossa. The remainder of the examination, including chest auscultation, is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step towards establishing a diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
There are many different causes for enlargement of cervical lymph nodes. These include malignancy and infection. Examples of malignancy include lymphomas or spread from an upper gastrointestinal or pulmonary malignancy. Bacterial infections could be acute or chronic. This migrant patient from a TB-endemic country presents with painless cervical lymphadenopathy and constitutional symptoms (night sweats, weight loss, fatigue) highly suspicious for tuberculous lymphadenitis.

Fine needle aspiration is the most appropriate initial test as it provides material for multiple diagnostic tests including microscopy, culture, PCR and histopathology. The presence of caseous granulomatous inflammation is characteristic of tuberculosis. The aspirate should be sent for acid-fast bacilli staining, mycobacterial culture, and nucleic acid amplification testing.

Chest X-ray may be normal in up to 75% of patients with tuberculous lymphadenitis. While chest imaging is important in the full work-up, a normal chest X-ray does not exclude the diagnosis and should not delay diagnostic sampling.

CT scan chest and abdomen may be useful to assess for other sites of disease such as mediastinal lymphadenopathy or abdominal involvement, but is not the appropriate first step before obtaining a tissue diagnosis. In addition, this is a young patient and malignancy is much less likely than chronic infection.

Mantoux test (tuberculin skin test) has poor specificity in BCG-vaccinated individuals and may be falsely negative in immunocompromised patients. It cannot distinguish latent from active TB and is therefore not helpful in diagnosis.

Interferon gamma release assay tests like QuantiFERON-TB Gold, while more specific than skin testing, also cannot distinguish between latent and active TB. These tests are more useful for screening rather than diagnosis of active disease.

After obtaining diagnostic samples, the patient will require a full work-up including chest imaging and HIV testing. Treatment typically involves a standard multi-drug anti-tuberculous regimen for 6-9 months.
learningPoints: Cervical tuberculous lymphadenitis (CTL) is the most common extrapulmonary finding seen in patients with disseminated TB.
Fine needle aspiration is the initial diagnostic test of choice for suspected tuberculous lymphadenitis as it provides material for multiple diagnostic tests.
Constitutional symptoms and lymphadenopathy in migrants from TB-endemic regions should prompt consideration of tuberculosis.
references: Knox J, Lane G, Wong J, Trevan P, Karunajeewa H. Diagnosis of tuberculous lymphadenitis using fine needle aspiration biopsy. Internal Medicine Journal. 2012;42(9):1029-1036.
Fontanilla J, Barnes A, von Reyn C. Current Diagnosis and Management of Peripheral Tuberculous Lymphadenitis. Clinical Infectious Diseases. 2011;53(6):555-562.
